coredata.dc:description: Plant transpiration is a ubiquitous process that continuously release water vapor to the atmosphere, which is an untapped resource of water. Hygroscopic sorbents are potential candidates to address water scarcity through efficient solar-driven atmospheric water harvesting (AWH). However, the composite sorbents containing multiple components are typically toxic and environment harmful, with low photothermal efficiency and poor cyclic stability. Herein, we utilize all natural plant-derived precursors for one-step synthesis of phosphate-polymer-wrapped carbon dots (PWCD) with multi-carbonized cores inside and wrapped polymer chains in the surrounding. Without need to combine other materials, the single all-in-one PWCD particle exhibits multiple functions including low toxicity, biocompatible, high moisture absorption, low-energy desorption, high photothermal temperature, excellent cyclic stability and antibacterial properties. The PWCD can absorb water vapor across a wide range from 10% to 90% RH, with water uptake ranging from 0.11 to 3.23 gwater/gabsorbents. Furthermore, PWCD also shows excellent photothermal desorption ability, with final photothermal temperature surpass 100 °C for in situ sterilization. The presence of wrapped polymer prevents PWCD from aggregation and agglomeration, enhancing their cyclic stability. As the results, the biocompatible PWCD was successfully applied for water harvest from plant transpiration, producing 0.51 g·g−1 per cycle near Epipremnum aureum while keeping plant fresh.

HRTEM image of PWCD was synthesized under different conditions (a) Effect of varying reaction temperatures (100, 140, 180, 220 °C) on the structure of PWCD; (b) Effect of varying IP-6 concentration (0, 0.005, 0.10, 0.5 g/mL) on the structure of PWCD.

PWCD water vapor sorption/desorption performance (a) Water vapor adsorption isotherms of PWCD and pectin-CD at different RH; (b) Sorption kinetics of PWCD at 25 °C and 20%, 50% and 80% RH, respectively; (c) Cycling performance of PWCD with sorption conditions set at 80 % RH and desorption temperature of 75 °C; (d) Desorption kinetics of saturated PWCD, LiCl solution and pure water in an oven at 75 °C; (e) Evaporation behavior and thermogravimetric analysis of LiCl solution; (f) Evaporation behavior and thermogravimetric analysis of PWCD; (g) Evaporation behavior of water; (h) FTIR spectra of PWCD and PWCD-80; (i) Fitted Raman spectra of the PWCD in the region of O–H stretching.

AWH performance of PWCD (a) Photo of carbon dots containers after washing; The black in pectin-CD container indicate the presence of CDs agglomeration. (b) Photo of the AWH device; (c) Cycling performance of PWCD at 80 % RH; (d) Agar plate photographs of bacterial colonies of E. coli and E. coli treat with 1 mg/mL PWCD and varying light durations; (e) Corresponding number of E. coli colonies after treatments; (f) Images of culture dish growth with PWCD extracted water, showing the ability for sterilization; (g) Collected water and corresponding ion concentration; (h) Photo of water collected.

AWH performance of PWCD (a) Photos of water harvest from plant transpiration and humidity change monitoring; (b) Water release rate of Epipremnum aureum under three conditions: under sunlight, outdoor without sunlight and indoor; (c) RH changes in the display box containing the Epipremnum aureum; (d) Water collection cycle performance of PWCD during transpiration of Epipremnum aureum; (e) The RH of the sealed plastic bag containing strawberries with and without PWCD for 82 h; (f) Water collection cycle performance of PWCD during strawberry storage. (g) Digital images of strawberries before (left) and after (right) stored in the sealed plastic bag with PWCD for 82 h and without PWCD for 82 h.
(a) The preparation process of PWCD; (b) AWH mechanism of PWCD.

head.ce:title: One-step fabrication of all-in-one biocompatible polymer wrapping carbon dots for efficient atmospheric water harvesting from plants

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Plant transpiration is a ubiquitous process that continuously release water vapor to the atmosphere, which is an untapped resource of water. Hygroscopic sorbents are potential candidates to address water scarcity through efficient solar-driven atmospheric water harvesting (AWH). However, the composite sorbents containing multiple components are typically toxic and environment harmful, with low photothermal efficiency and poor cyclic stability. Herein, we utilize all natural plant-derived precursors for one-step synthesis of phosphate-polymer-wrapped carbon dots (PWCD) with multi-carbonized cores inside and wrapped polymer chains in the surrounding. Without need to combine other materials, the single all-in-one PWCD particle exhibits multiple functions including low toxicity, biocompatible, high moisture absorption, low-energy desorption, high photothermal temperature, excellent cyclic stability and antibacterial properties. The PWCD can absorb water vapor across a wide range from 10% to 90% RH, with water uptake ranging from 0.11 to 3.23 gwater/gabsorbents. Furthermore, PWCD also shows excellent photothermal desorption ability, with final photothermal temperature surpass 100 °C for in situ sterilization. The presence of wrapped polymer prevents PWCD from aggregation and agglomeration, enhancing their cyclic stability. As the results, the biocompatible PWCD was successfully applied for water harvest from plant transpiration, producing 0.51 g·g−1 per cycle near Epipremnum aureum while keeping plant fresh.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Freshwater is an indispensable need for the sustenance of life for human beings [1]. Therefore, addressing the increasing demand for freshwater has become critically important. As the global population continues to rise and water sources face increasing pollution, ensuring access to sufficient and safe freshwater has become an urgent challenge [2]. In its latest annual risk report, the World Economic Forum identifies the water crisis as a significant global risk due to its potential impact [3]. Atmospheric water harvesting, which extracts drinkable water from air, has emerged as a promising technology for freshwater production [4].

However, a frequently overlooked and sustainable source of water is the moisture present in the air above plant surfaces. Plant transpiration is a ubiquitous process in which plants release water to the atmosphere in the form of moisture or water vapor, making it easier to access this untapped resource of water [5]. It is estimated that the transpiration could produces 62,000 ± 8,000 km3 of water per year to the atmosphere [6], corresponding to more than 60% of the world's freshwater. This transpiration occurs all the time at the surface of leaves, fruits and vegetables, either on the plant or after postharvest [7]. The in-situ capture and utilization of these moisture to produce clean water would be of great significance to solve the water crisis.

Recently, hygroscopic adsorbent-based atmospheric water harvesting (AWH) that captures water vapor and employs solar-driven desorption is considered a promising technology for clean water production. Various sorbents have been developed, providing substantial material candidates for AWH, such as zeolites, MOF, molecular sieves, silica gel and LiCl [8,9]. To enhance their AWH performance, many composite materials have been developed by combining sorbents with various porous matrices, thermo-responsive materials and photothermal materials [10,11]. However, most of these reported composite materials are highly toxic (e.g. PNIPAAM), environmentally harmful, and non-biocompatible with high fabrication cost. The use of multiple harmful reagents in sorbents raises more concerns about safety of harvested water [12]. On the other hand, pollutants like microorganisms, such as microorganisms are commonly present in aerosol moisture or may adhere to atmospheric water harvesting (AWH) devices, posing a risk of contamination in the harvested water. Therefore, ensuring the safety of water produced through AWH is a critical consideration. Furthermore, these hygroscopic sorbents tends to agglomerate and form salt crystals, leading to issues such as significantly decreased water uptake, incomplete water release and low cyclic stability [11]. For example, adsorbents (LiCl) loaded on porous hydrogel are susceptible to salt leakage and salting-out effect, affecting their actual recycle stability.

Water desorption is another key factor influencing the efficiency of AWH. Traditional hygroscopic sorbents, such as LiCl, often demand substantial energy input and extended durations to release the adsorbed water partially, thereby limiting their overall efficiency. To achieve highly efficient solar-driven AWH, many photothermal components were added in composite materials to increase photothermal temperature. However, these AWH sorbents usually suffer from suboptimal energy utilization efficiency because the hygroscopic materials and photothermal materials are usually separated, resulting in heat loss and inefficient heat transfer [13]. Incorporating external photothermal materials into adsorbents for water release can decrease water uptake and complicate fabrication, creating additional challenges for the efficiency and scalability of atmospheric water harvesting systems.

Therefore, it is crucial to develop a nature-derived single material that combines low toxicity, high biocompatibility, excellent water vapor absorption, low-energy desorption, high photothermal efficiency, outstanding cyclic stability, and in situ disinfection capabilities. Carbon dots (CDs) are carbon-based nanoparticles typically smaller than 10 nm in size. They can be synthesized from bio-derived precursors, offering advantages such as excellent biocompatibility, low cytotoxicity, and exceptional photoelectric properties, making them highly versatile for various applications. The assembled CDs at high concentrations enhance visible and near-infrared absorbance [14], enabling efficient heat generation to facilitate water release. However, CDs tend to aggregate irreversibly, resulting in significantly decreased water uptake, incomplete water release and low cyclic stability.

In this study, we synthesized phosphate-polymer-wrapped carbon dots (PWCD) for water harvest from plant transpiration, through one-step hydrothermal fabrication using pectin and inositol hexaphosphate (IP-6) (Scheme 1
a). Both precursors are biocompatible and widely found in nature plants. The synthesized PWCD shows core–shell structures with multi-carbonized dots core inside and abundant polymer chains wrapped in the outer layer. Without the need to combine other materials, the single PWCD exhibits multiple functions, including low toxicity, excellent biocompatibility, high moisture absorption, outstanding photothermal conversion, fast desorption, excellent cyclic stability, and antibacterial properties (Scheme 1 b). Benefitting from the plentiful hydrophilic groups on PWCD serving as moisture adsorption sites, it can adsorb water vapor across a wide range from 10% to 90% RH, with water uptake ranging from 0.11 to 3.23 gwater/gabsorbents. Meanwhile, PWCD can desorb 80% of water within 36 min at 75 °C, which is even much faster than that of pure water evaporation. Furthermore, PWCD also shows excellent photothermal conversion ability, with final photothermal temperature surpass 100 °C under one sunlight. The presence of wrapped polymer chains prevents PWCD from aggregation and agglomeration, thus enhancing the cyclic stability without deterioration of performance. As a result, PWCD demonstrates a promising AWH capacity, capable of producing 2.04 L·kg−1·day−1 of clean water at approximately 80 % RH. Additionally, the final PWCD was successfully utilized for water harvesting from plant transpiration, yielding 0.51 g·g−1 per cycle near Epipremnum aureum while maintaining the plant's freshness.

Pectin with galacturonic acid ≥ 74.0% (dried basis) and Lithium chloride (LiCl, 99%) were purchased from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Technology Co., ltd. (Shanghai, China). Inositol hexaphosphate was purchased from Aladdin (China). Hydrochloric acid (HCl, ACS reagent) and ethanol (99.5%) were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd (China).

Phosphate-polymer-wrapped carbon dots (PWCD) were synthesized via a one-step hydrothermal method. Initially, 200 mg of pectin powder was dispersed in 50 mL of distilled water and stirred at room temperature for 20 min using a magnetic stirrer. Inositol hexaphosphate was then added to the pectin solution to achieve various concentrations (0, 0.001, 0.05, 0.1, and 0.5 g/mL). The mixture was subsequently reacted at 220 °C for 5 h. For the specific synthesis, a solution with an inositol hexaphosphate concentration of 0.1 mg/mL was transferred into a polytetrafluoroethylene-lined autoclave and reacted at temperatures of 100, 140, 180, or 220 °C for 5 h. The resulting solution was filtered through a 0.22 μm polyethersulfone (PES) filter membrane. The filtered solution was dried at 75 °C in an oven, dissolved in anhydrous ethanol, and centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 15 min. This process resulted in the formation of a PWCD ethanol solution, which was stored at 4 °C for future use. Finally, the PWCD ethanol solution was dried at 75 °C, yielding the prepared PWCD for subsequent applications.

Pectin Carbon Dots (pectin-CD) was synthesized with modifications to a previously reported method [15]. Specifically, 0.2 g of pectin was dispersed in 50 mL of distilled water and stirred for 20 min using a magnetic stirrer. The solution pH was then adjusted to 3 using a 1 M HCl solution. The prepared solution was carefully transferred into a polytetrafluoroethylene-lined high-pressure reactor and subjected to a controlled reaction at 160 °C for 5 h. After the reaction, the solution was allowed to cool to room temperature and filtered through a 0.22 μm polyethersulfone (PES) filter membrane to remove residual impurities. The filtered solution was then pre-frozen at −80 °C for 12 h to prepare for freeze-drying. The freeze-drying process was conducted in a vacuum freeze dryer, resulting in a dried powder. Finally, the obtained powder was washed with anhydrous ethanol to remove any remaining impurities. The washed solution was centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 15 min to produce the pectin-CD ethanol solution, which was preserved for subsequent use.

The morphology and microstructure of the samples were observed using a high-resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM) (JEM-2100F, Japan) and Scanning Electron Microscope (SEM) (GeminiSEM 300). Fourier-transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were acquired within the wavenumber range of 4000 to 400 cm−1 at a spectral resolution of 4 cm−1 (NicoLET iS50FT-IR, Thermo Scientific, USA). Zeta potential were recorded by a Zetasizer Nano ZS90 (Malvern, UK). UV–Vis spectra ranging from 200 nm to 700 nm were obtained using a UV–visible spectrophotometer (VarianCary-500 spectrophotometer，Shimadzu UV 3600). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was employed to analyze the elemental composition of the samples (Thermo Scientific Nexsa, USA). Thermal gravimetric analysis and adsorption/desorption behaviors were investigated using a simultaneous thermal analyzer, operating with a gas flow rate of 100 mL·min−1 and a heating rate of 10 °C·min−1 (TGA/DSC3+, Mettler Toledo, Switzerland). The transmittance of the samples was analyzed using a UV–visible-near-infrared spectrophotometer (Lambda950，NatureSci Technologies Corporati，USA). Temperature infrared images were recorded using an infrared thermal imaging camera (H13pro，HIKVISION,China). Photothermal desorption was conducted by simulating sunlight using a xenon lamp (PLS-SXE300+,Perfectlight Technology Co.,Ltd. China). The ion concentration in the collected water was determined using a plasma mass spectrometer (NexION 300XX，PerknElmer，USA).

PWCD was evenly spread in a 35 mm petri dish and placed in a constant temperature and humidity chamber to evaluate its moisture absorption capacity. The pre-dried samples at 75 °C were positioned in a constant temperature and humidity chamber to assess their moisture absorption capability at 50%, 60%, 70%, 80%, and 90% RH, as well as at 25 °C (MLR-352H-PC PHC Corporation，Japan). PWCD saturated at different humidity was named PWCD-50, PWCD-60, PWCD-70, PWCD-D-80 and PWCD-90 respectively. Weight changes were recorded using an electronic balance, and the water uptake was calculated by Equation 1:

where m1 is the mass of PWCD after hygroscopicity, and m0 is the initial mass of PWCD when it is completely dried before hygroscopicity.

For sample regeneration and cycling tests, PWCD was first placed at 50% RH and 25 °C to absorb water, followed by desorption in an oven at 75 °C. This adsorption–desorption cycle was performed 10 times. The same batch of PWCD was exposed to 80% RH and 25 °C to absorb water and regenerated in an oven at 75 °C, followed by another 10 adsorption–desorption cycles. In total, 20 adsorption–desorption cycles were conducted to confirm the regeneration capability and cyclic stability of PWCD.

The evaporation comparison experiment with LiCl and pure water was conducted by adding a specified amount of each sample into a 35 mm petri dish, spreading them evenly, and ensuring that both PWCD and LiCl absorbed approximately 3 g of water. And the Evaporation behavior and thermogravimetric analysis of PWCD-80 and LiCl-80P (the same concentration of PWCD-80) and distilled water at 75 °C also conducted.

PWCD was placed in a humidity-controlled chamber at 50% RH, 80% and 25 °C for 12 h to absorb water to simulate solar-driven desorption. A glass dish containing approximately 2 g of PWCD (with a diameter of 60 mm) was placed in a foam container, and a windscreen was used around it to minimize heat convection. A xenon lamp was used to simulate sunlight to irradiate the samples, and the mass and temperature changes of the samples were monitored every 2 min. The mass change, evaporation rate and evaporation efficiency of the sample were calculated by the following equations:

Where ma is the mass of the initial PWCD, mb is the mass of the PWCD after 12 h of water absorption, and mc is the mass after sunlight irradiation for a period of time. S is the surface area of the adsorbent in the container during photothermal desorption, and T is the duration of solar irradiation.

Bacterial culture: Escherichia coli (E. coli, ATCC 25922) and Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus, ATCC 29213) were transferred to an LB liquid medium and incubated at 37 °C with shaking at 220 rpm for 10 h. The bacterial suspension was then diluted to 1 × 107 CFU/mL using a 0.9% (w/v) saline solution.

Antibacterial activity measurement: S. aureus and E. coli were selected as model bacteria for the inhibition test, and the antibacterial effect of PWCD was evaluated in Luria-Bertani (LB) agar. The influence of light exposure time on the antibacterial effect was also investigated. The light source is a xenon lamp that simulates the one sunlight (1000 W). The bacterial suspension was treated by 1.0 mg/mL PWCD, and different irradiation times (0 min, 10 min, 20 min). The bacterial concentration was diluted to approximately 107 CFU/mL using saline. PWCD and bacterial suspension were mixed, one group was placed under xenon lamp for light (0, 10, 20 min), and the other group was placed in a biological incubator for dark culture (0, 10, 20 min). Bacterial suspension without PWCD was used as control and viable bacteria were counted by the plate counting method.

An CCK-8 assay was performed to verify the cytotoxicity of PWCD, in which HT-29 cells were selected as representatives. Cells were first added in 96-well plates and cultured at 37 °C for 24 h. Then, PWCD solutions at different concentrations (0, 0.05. 0.1, 0.2, 0.4, 0.8, 1.6 mg/mL) were added to the plate and continued to incubate. After 6 h, 100 μL fresh cell medium and 10 μL CCK-8 solution were added, which was incubated for another 1 h. Finally, the absorbance at 492 nm was measured.

The AWH system of the samples comprises a quartz glass water evaporation collection device, featuring grooves on three sides at the bottom for water collection and a glass top inclined at a 45-degree angle to facilitate the runoff of condensed water droplets. PWCD was placed at 50 %RH and 25 °C to absorb water for 3 h, and then desorbed by a xenon lamp for 2 h. The adsorption/desorption cycles were performed 10 times, and the water was collected. After 10 times of water absorption at 50% RH, the humidity was adjusted to 80% RH for 5 h of water absorption, xenon lamp simulated a solar light desorption for 2 h, and then 10 adsorption/desorption cycles were carried out, and the water obtained by desorption was collected.

Water collection by plant transpiration: The Epipremnum aureum and PWCD were placed together in a transparent display box, and the humidity change in the box was monitored using a hygrometer. At regular intervals, the PWCD was removed and placed at the device of Fig. 5 under simulate sunlight irradiation for water collection.

Fruit preservation and water collection: fresh strawberries (about 130 g) were placed in polyethylene plastic bags (22 cm in length and 32 cm in width), and pre-dried PWCD (about 6 g) placed in plastic petri dishes was added to the plastic bags. At the same time, a small hygrometer was placed to monitor the humidity change in the plastic bags. It was immediately sealed and placed in an indoor environment of about 25 °C and 22% relative humidity. In addition, sealed bags without PWCD were stored under the same conditions for strawberry preparation as a control.

For the preservation of blueberries, fresh blueberries (about 30 g) were placed in polyethylene storage bags of 12 cm and 17 cm in length and width respectively, and PWCD (about 1 g) was added to the plastic bag after pre-drying placed in a plastic Petri dish. At the same time, a small hygrometer was placed to monitor the humidity change in the plastic bag. It was immediately sealed and placed in an indoor environment of about 25 °C and 27% relative humidity. In addition, sealed bags without PWCD were stored under the same conditions for blueberry preparation as a control.

PWCD is synthesized through a one-step hydrothermal method using pectin and IP-6, both of which are biocompatible and derived from natural plants. Pectin is one kind of dietary fiber that widely existed in the mesoglea of various plant cell [16]. While the IP-6 is ubiquitous in eukaryotes and regulates a plenty of cellular functions [17]. Under high temperatures of 220 °C, the intense contact and collision of pectin and IP-6 molecules result in carbonized dots core with abundant polymer chains wrapped in the outer layer. High-angle annular dark-field (HAADF) TEM images distinctly reveal the morphology of PWCD with many carbonized cores inside and wrapped polymer chains in the surrounding (Fig. 1
a,b). High-resolution TEM revealed that the carbonized cores exhibited a crystalline structure with an atomic lattice fringe of 0.21 nm, which is similar to the (100) in-plane lattice spacing of graphite [15] (Fig. 1 c). Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images also reveal that PWCD are indeed spherical particles (Fig. S1). To the best of our knowledge, this is the first time to one-step synthesize the multi-carbonized cores embedded in polymer particles. In Fig. S2, the peak at 1332 cm−1 corresponds to the D band, while the G band is located at 1591 cm−1 [14,18]. The relative ratio of the D to G bands (ID/IG) is 1.29 The sizes of entire PWCD particles are measured to be 90.6 ± 17.7 nm, while their carbonization cores are 7.7 ± 1.3 nm, respectively, indicating the well-crystallized core of PWCD (Fig. S3). To further confirm the core–shell structures, line scan analysis was conducted. As shown in Fig. 1 d, the bright cores in the PWCD were composed of high amount of C, indicating a highly carbonated core. Meanwhile, the high intensities of P and O in the PWCD suggests the wrapped polymer chain was rich in phosphate groups (Fig. 1 d). This is also consistent with the result of P element mapping (Fig. S4).

To further investigate the elemental composition and functional groups of PWCD, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) and Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) analyses were performed. As shown in Fig. 1 e, the XPS survey confirmed that PWCD is primarily composed of carbon (C), oxygen (O), and phosphorus (P), with atomic ratios of 29.90%, 55.07%, and 15.03%, respectively. In the high-resolution C 1 s spectrum, three peaks at 284.7 eV (C=C/C-C), 286.6 eV (C-O), and 287.8 eV (C=O) was observed, which serve as typical carbon dots characteristic signal (Fig. 1 f) [19]. The peaks at 531.37 eV, 533.47 eV, and 532.13 eV in the high-resolution O 1 s spectrum are assigned to C-O, P=O, and P-O in PWCD, respectively (Fig. 1 g) [20]. And the signal peak observed at 134.8 eV in the high-resolution P 2p spectrum indicates the presence of phosphorus-oxygen (P-O) bonds (Fig. 1 h) [20].

FTIR measurements provide more evidence about the functional group of PWCD. Compared with conventional carbon dots made from only pectin (pectin-CD), new peaks of P=O stretching at 1131 cm−1, P-O-H stretching at 888 cm−1 and PO4 deformation vibration at 490 cm−1 in PWCD, which are attributed to the successful phosphate group modification (Fig. 1 i) [21]. Furthermore, compared with IP-6, the peaks at 802 cm−1 and 670 cm−1 (corresponding to C-H of ring) was disappeared in PWCD, indicating that the carboatomic ring structure from IP to 6 was destroyed after hydrothermal reaction [22]. Meanwhile, the broad C-H peak of PWCD (from 2510 cm−1 to 3100 cm−1) is stronger than that of pectin-CD and IP-6, confirming the formation of long-chain alkanes. These results indicate that the PWCD contain abundant of polymer chain with phosphoric acid functional groups. To further clarify the structure of PWCD, the 31P nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) spectra of PWCD and IP-6 was conducted to predict the structure of polymer chains. The 31P nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) spectra of PWCD and IP-6 are shown in Fig. S5. The IP-6 spectrum revealed six distinct phosphorus peaks, while PWCD exhibited a single peak at 0.64 ppm, indicating that the phosphorus atoms in various chemical environments of phytic acid were homogenized. This suggests that the six-membered ring structure of IP-6 breaks under high-temperature conditions, leading to the formation of a new phosphate-rich long polymer chain, as illustrated in the molecular formula in Fig. S5. The phosphorus atoms in this structure were transitioned to the same chemical environment with the appearance of a single peak. This long-chain alkane is enriched with numerous phosphate groups in its structure, and the influence of other groups on the phosphorus chemical shift is negligible due to the extended length of the polymer chain [23].

The synthesized PWCD exhibits an optically black appearance with outstanding broadband light absorption, exceeding 97% across the entire solar spectrum range of 200 to 2500 nm (Fig. 1 j,k). This remarkable broadband light absorption is attributed to the self-assembly of PWCD molecules at high concentrations, which enhances their optical properties. In the assembly PWCD with multi-carbonized cores, the respective energy levels in highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) and lowest unoccupied molecular orbit (LUMO) can overlap spatially, resulting in a lower band gap for enhanced visible-NIR absorption [24]. Given these properties, PWCD emerges as a promising contender for AWH solar-driven desorption.

To further elucidate the formation mechanism of PWCD, the effects of reaction temperature and IP-6 concentration were investigated in detail. At a reaction temperature of 100 °C, no discernible carbonized core was observed (Fig. 2
a). However, when the temperature reached 140 °C, the formation of the carbonized core structure began. As the reaction temperature increased further, the number of carbonized cores in the PWCD progressively grew, and by 220 °C, a distinct multi-core–shell structure was evident. Furthermore, we investigated the impact of IP-6 concentration on PWCD structure formation. When only pectin was used, the result was the formation of pristine carbon dots (pectin-CD) without any polymer chains wrapped around the outer layer, highlighting the critical role of IP-6 in creating the unique polymer-wrapped structure of PWCD. (Fig. 2 b). With the addition of IP-6, the phosphate polymer wrapping layer begins to form. However, when the IP-6 concentration was further increased to 0.5 g/mL, the number of polymer particles containing carbonized core in the product decreased and the particle size distribution was also uneven (123.1 ± 86.9 nm). Therefore, 0.1 g/mL IP-6 was selected as the reaction concentration. The above results demonstrate that under high temperatures of 220 °C, the intense collision of pectin could form carbonized dots. Meanwhile, polymerization occurs between the IP-6 and pectin to form wrapped polymer chains at the out layer.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Water vapor adsorption and desorption kinetics of PWCD
ce:section.ce:para:
The synthesized PWCD possess excellent water vapor absorption properties. The hygroscopic groups, primarily phosphate groups, offer numerous hygroscopic sites along the polymer chain (Fig. S6). The water uptake gradually increases from 0.11 g·g−1 at 10% RH to 0.67 g·g−1 at 60% RH and reach 3.23 g·g−1 at 90% RH (Fig. 3
a). For contrast, conventional pectin-CD exhibits negligible moisture absorption at RH less than 50%, and only water uptake of 0.51 g·g−1 at 80% RH. Besides, the IP-6 also displays lower hygroscopicity at 80% RH, with water uptake of 0.52 g·g−1 (Fig. S7). The increased water uptake is mainly attributed to the abundant active sites provided by phosphate functional groups present in the PWCD particles with high specific ratio. At low humidity levels, the abundant phosphate groups on PWCD act as strong nucleation sites for water molecule adsorption [25]. Phosphate groups (−PO4) are highly hydrophilic due to their ability to form hydrogen bonds with water molecules. This characteristic attracts water vapor in the air, promoting condensation on the PWCD surface. The polar nature of phosphate groups lowers the energy barrier for water nucleation, allowing water molecules to aggregate into droplets more efficiently [26]. As the humidity increases, water molecules continuously diffuse inward due to concentration gradients, leading to successive layer-by-layer binding until saturation [27]. Once condensed, the hydrogen bonds between the phosphate groups and the water molecules stabilize the droplets, preventing premature detachment. This is crucial for effective water harvesting. The amount of phosphate functional groups within the polymer wrapping enhances the wettability of the material, creating a synergistic effect with the nano-structured surface morphology of PWCD to maximize water capture efficiency. These results indicate that PWCD with core inside and wrapped polymers outside, possess excellent water absorption properties across a wide range from 10% to 90% RH. We further measured the adsorption kinetics curves at different RH. At 20%, 50% and 80% RH, PWCD can reach 80% of the saturated water absorption in 30 min, 60 min and 180 min, respectively (Fig. 3 b). In contrast, pectin-CD exhibited limited moisture absorption, even at a high humidity of 80% RH (Fig. S8). The fast water adsorption rate may be benefit from the individual PWCD particle with high specific surface ratio without aggregation. The presence of wrapped polymer chains prevents PWCD from aggregation and agglomeration, thus expanding the contact area between the hydrophilic group and moisture [28]. The zeta potential of PWCD was measured to be 20.17 ± 1.26 mV, which was larger than that of pectin-CD (0.69 ± 0.01 mV), indicating the excellent dispersibility of PWCD (Fig. S9). As a result, PWCD exhibited stable water absorption cyclic stability for more than 10 cycles without deterioration of performance (Fig. 3 c and Fig. S10). As shown in Fig. S11, after ten adsorption–desorption cycles, the structure of PWCD was further examined using HRTEM after it was dissolved in ethanol. The HRTEM images reveal that PWCD maintains its core–shell structure, and line scan analysis confirms that the polymer chain structure of PWCD remains intact. This demonstrates the long-term stability of PWCD. In addition, we conducted XPS analysis of PWCD before and after ten water collection cycles to assess any potential degradation or changes. As shown in Fig. S12, the elemental binding of PWCD remained largely unchanged before and after the cycles, confirming the long-term stability and safety of PWCD.

Desorption performance is another important indicator of AWH materials. At 75 °C, PWCD desorbed 80% of the adsorbed water within 1 h (Fig. 3 d), whereas LiCl desorbed only about 40% under identical conditions. Upon reaching equilibrium, PWCD achieved complete water desorption, while LiCl retained 40% of the adsorbed water, indicating the presence of strongly bonded water in LiCl. Interesting, the desorption rate of water in PWCD was also larger than that of pure water in both high and low temperature. As shown in Fig. 3 d and Figs. S13-14, PWCD exhibited higher evaporation efficiency than pure water and pectin-CD at both high (75 °C) and low temperatures (25 °C) throughout the desorption process. These results demonstrate that PWCD outperforms both LiCl solution and pure water in desorption performance. To clarify the desorption mechanism, thermal gravimetric/differential scanning calorimetry (TG/DSC) was employed to evaluate the heat flow distribution of LiCl solution, distilled water and PWCD. As shown in Fig. 3 e, the desorption process of LiCl solution exhibits three distinct evaporation peaks at approximately 65 °C, 95 °C, and 153 °C, corresponding to the evaporation from the bulk solution, crystallization, and chemical desorption of LiCl, respectively [29]. This suggests that LiCl forms salt hydrates with water, necessitating greater energy input for the desorption process. In contrast, PWCD shows a single primary evaporation peak at 66 °C, which is lower than the evaporation temperature of pure water (Fig. 3 f,g). The TG curve indicates that the majority of the water in the PWCD solution (> 90%) is easily evaporated unbound water. This indicates that the binding enthalpy between water molecules and the surface of PWCD is relatively lower, which facilitates desorption at reduced temperatures. As shown in Fig. S15, The PWCD pre-dried at 75 °C and 100 °C show completely water desorption, confirming the results. These results confirm that PWCD solution can desorb at lower temperatures with lower energy input, consistent with the phenomena observed in Fig. 3 d. The lower evaporation temperature can be attributed to the weakening of hydrogen bonding interactions among outer layer water molecules on PWCD as the number of adsorbed water molecules increases and their polarity changes.

The hydrogen bonding in PWCD after moisture absorption was analyzed using FTIR (Fig. 3 h). The broad band at 3200–3600 cm−1, attributed to the O–H stretching vibration, increased significantly after saturation at 80 % RH, indicating that free water molecules form strong interactions with PWCD surface groups via a stable P-O–H bonding network [30]. Raman spectroscopy was conducted to analyze the hydrogen bonding in saturated PWCD systems, as shown in Fig. 3 i. The constrained water in the saturated PWCD system was categorized into three types of hydrogen bonds: strong, mediate, and weak [31]. Strong hydrogen bonds represent bound water, which is more resistant to evaporation, while mediate hydrogen bonds behave similarly to bulk water. Weak hydrogen bonds, characterized by weaker interactions with surfaces or ions and fewer hydrogen bonds with other water molecules, primarily contribute to the lower evaporation temperature in PWCD. The proportions of strong, mediate, and weak bonds were 11.17%, 45.63%, and 43.21%, respectively. The high proportion of moderate and weak bonding water with low hydrogen bonds reduces the enthalpy of evaporation, thereby enabling a higher evaporation rate. Raman spectra at different adsorption times showed that PWCD had a lower proportion of strong hydrogen bonds compared to pectin-CD (Fig. S16). This result shows that PWCD has excellent desorption performance. The remarkable water adsorption–desorption performance and cycling stability of biocompatible PWCD highlight its sustainable potential for AWH.

To achieve solar-driven AWH, efficient photothermal evaporation is one of the essential conditions. The photothermal conversion property of PWCD was investigated under simulated sunlight. The temperature changes of both PWCD-50 (saturated at 50% RH) and PWCD-80 (saturated at 80% RH) were recorded using a thermal infrared imager, while the mass alterations were continuously monitored in real-time with an electronic balance (Fig. S17). The temperatures of PWCD-50 and PWCD-80 rose rapidly, reaching 73.9 °C and 61.1 °C, respectively, within the first 15 min (Fig. 4
a). The final temperatures stabilized above 100 °C, higher than that of pectin-CD, demonstrating PWCD's excellent photothermal conversion efficiency and effective thermal management (Fig. 4 b and Fig. S18). These excellent photothermal properties of PWCD was superior to those reported in the literature to date [4,32–39] (Fig. 4 c). Rapid photothermal heating performance of PWCD arise from the enhanced NIR absorbance due to their self-assembly (Fig. 1 k), which improves photothermal conversion performance for water release. It has been revealed that the increase of oxygen-phosphorus content on the surface of carbon dots will lead to the formation of numerous trap-state energy levels, resulting in non-radiative recombination and higher photothermal conversion efficiency [24,40,41]. The polymer wrapping on the carbon dots enhances the thermal conductivity of PWCDs while minimizing heat dissipation. This ensures that the absorbed energy is effectively localized, enabling rapid temperature increases. PWCD-50 and PWCD-80 are capable of desorbing more than 80% of the water within 40 min and 60 min, respectively. After this, the energy from light is primarily used to heat the material itself, leading to a maximum temperature exceeding 100 °C. In these experiments, a glass dish containing approximately 2 g of PWCD (with a diameter of 60 mm) was placed in a foam container, and a windscreen was used around it to minimize heat convection.

With its outstanding photothermal conversion performance, saturated PWCD can release water much faster. Heat transfer from photothermal layer to hygroscopic materials was a critical step that determined the energy utilization efficiency. In previous work, the hygroscopic materials and photothermal materials are usually separated, resulting in heat loss and inefficient heat transfer [13]. Incorporating photothermal materials like carbon nanotubes [4,32] and pyrrole onto adsorbents for water release can reduce water uptake, and decrease overall heat transfer efficiency [13,35]. In this work, the hygroscopic material and photothermal material are indeed one nanomaterial (i.e. PWCD), which ensure efficient and direct energy transfer from light to adsorbed water molecule, thereby minimizing heat dissipation. The mass change of PWCD showed an almost linear decline in the first 20 min, and then gradually slowed down, with maximum evaporation rates measured to be 1.34 kg·m−2·h−1 (PWCD-50) and 1.60 kg·m−2·h−1 (PWCD-80), respectively (Fig. 4 d,e). This high evaporation rate obtained in this work is attributed to the single absorbent with high photothermal capacity and fast desorption performance. In conventional AWH systems, the heat was generated on the photothermal layer, which must then be transferred to the sorbents. Due to heat transfer loss in these systems, the overall solar energy utilization efficiency is significantly reduced, as illustrated in Fig. S19 [13]. The hygroscopic and photothermal materials in PWCD are indeed one material, thus allows heat to be rapidly transferred from PWCD to the nearby adsorbed water for desorption with minimal heat loss shown in below. The desorption time for PWCD-50 and PWCD-80 to release 80% of the absorbed water is measured to be 36 min and 60 min, respectively (Fig. 4 f). Eventually, the adsorbed water can be completely released, suggesting the feasibility of solar-driven water release and collection. Thus, the exceptional hygroscopic, photothermal, and recycling properties, along with the low regeneration temperatures, endow PWCD with significant potential for applications in AWH.

ce:section.ce:section-title: AWH performance and biosafety of PWCD
ce:section.ce:para: PWCD exhibits outstanding redispersibility after several water absorbance and photothermal desorption cycling, which is crucial for its sustainable AWH. Conventional pectin-CD are prone to irreversible agglomeration due to intermolecular interactions like hydrogen bonding and π-π stacking under high concentration, hindering their sustainable and recyclable applications [42]. After 5 cycles of adsorption and desorption, the samples were washed with water to observe the adhesion of PWCD and pectin-CD to the container walls. pectin-CD exhibited noticeable agglomeration and settled at the bottom of the containers, as shown in Fig. 5 a. The agglomeration and settlement of pectin-CD making it hard to redistribute. However, in the PWCD, due to the abundance of polymer chain around PWCD with charged functional group, irreversible agglomeration of carbon dots was prevented, thereby ensuring robust stability during PWCD regeneration. So PWCD is highly dispersible in water, preventing accumulation and settling at the bottom of the petri dish. Therefore, PWCD can still be highly dispersed after a series of cyclic experiments.

To further validate the feasibility of AWH, water collection experiments were conducted using a custom-built AWH apparatus. The AWH system consists of a triangular box made of highly transparent quartz glass with grooves for water collection on three sides of the square (Fig. 5 b). PWCD was subjected to absorption under 50% RH or 80% RH within a humidity chamber with constant temperature, followed by desorption under one sunlight. PWCD-80 demonstrated stable performance over 10 adsorption–desorption cycles, with each cycle achieving an average water uptake of 1.19 g·g−1 and desorption of 1.00 g·g−1, resulting in a collected water yield of 0.68 g·g−1 (Fig. 5 c). At lower humidity (50% RH), PWCD-50 achieved a water collection rate of 0.26 g·g−1 per cycle, as shown in Fig. S20. The difference between water desorption and yield may be attributed to the airtight nature of device and residue water on the wall, which can be further addressed by device engineering. PWCD has a potential atmospheric water harvesting (AWH) capacity of producing 2.04 L·kg−1·day−1 and 1.30 L·kg−1·day−1 of clean water at ∼ 80% RH and ∼ 50% RH, respectively. It is noteworthy that in practical engineering applications, the actual clean water production of PWCD can be further augmented by incorporating fans, condensers, and adjusting the surface hydrophilicity/hydrophobicity of the AWH device.

The safety of water harvested from the atmosphere must be considered, as microorganisms are commonly present in aerosol moisture. With a high photothermal temperature near 100 °C, PWCD can efficiently sterilize the captured atmospheric moisture in situ, eliminating airborne pathogens. Fig. 5d,e and S21 shows that both the number of E. coli and S. aureus colonies in PWCD treatment groups decreased by about 5 log10 CFU/mL after 10 min of illumination and were completely inactivated after only 20 min of irradiation. Due to the excellent photocatalytic synergistic sterilization performance of PWCD, no bacterial colonies or any other pollutant were found in the clean water harvested through PWCD, ensuring the biosafety of the released clean water (Fig. 5 f). As shown in Fig. 5 g, we analyzed the collected water for common hazards and potential by-product concentrations from PWCD. The results indicated that the potential hazards were well below the standards established by the World Health Organization, confirming the material's stability and safety.

Moreover, the transfer of sorbent components to fresh water has been reported after AWH [43]. Since the PWCD are single sorbents derived from edible plant, the biological toxicity and biosafety concern can be significantly reduced compared to that of composite materials using diverse kinds of chemicals. Therefore, the PWCD sorbent itself exhibits excellent biocompatibility. As shown in Fig. S22, there was no significant difference in the effect of different concentrations of PWCD on cell viability, indicating good biocompatibility. All these results confirm the safety and reliability of the collected water and validate the clean water production capability of PWCD (Fig. 5 h).

The comparison of PWCD and other AWH materials in biocompatibility, contaminant removal, desorption and photothermal properties has shown in Table S1. First, as PWCD are plant-derived single sorbents, they pose significantly lower biological toxicity and biosafety risks compared to composite materials made with various chemicals. As demonstrated in Fig. S22, PWCD showed no toxicity to cell viability, confirming their excellent biocompatibility. Second, with the high photothermal temperature and light-driven photo-oxidation activity, PWCD can be also used for in situ efficient sterilization to kill airborne pathogens and environmental pollutant in the captured atmosphere moisture (Fig. 5 d-g and S23). Third, with its outstanding photothermal conversion performance, saturated PWCD can release water much faster. As shown in Table S1, under one sunlight irradiation, the temperature of PWCD-50 and PWCD-80 increase rapidly, and final temperature could be stabilized above 100 °C. With its outstanding photothermal conversion performance, saturated PWCD can release water much faster.

The plants on earth continuously undergo transpiration and respiration, which produce large amounts of water vapor and released into the surrounding air. It is estimated that the transpiration could produces 62,000 ± 8,000 km3 of water per year to the atmosphere, corresponding to about 30 % of total freshwater on earth. This released water has been overlooked and never effectively utilized. Therefore, utilizing this moisture to produce clean water is of great significance in addressing the water crisis. In this study, Epipremnum aureum was used as a model plant to harvest water from plant transpiration. (Fig. 6
a). Fig. 6 b shows the measured transpiration rate of plant Epipremnum aureum under sunlight, outdoor without sunlight, or indoor (Fig. S24). The individual plants undergo significant transpiration with maximum water release rate 13.3 g⋅h−1. Even when transferred to a place without sunlight, the release rate remained at 6.5 g⋅h−1, respectively. Due to the high transpiration rate, the relative humidity near the plant is consistently higher than ambient RH of external environment, as shown in Fig. 6 b. This further confirms that the plant continuously releases water into the atmosphere, causing the local humidity to quickly increase (Fig. 6 c). The PWCD sorbents were put next to the Epipremnum aureum to capture these in situ released vapors effectively (see photos in Fig. S25). The captured water was then harvested by exposing PWCD to sunlight for water collection and sorbents regeneration. As shown in Fig. 6 d, the PWCD for clean water collection in plants can stably perform 4 cycles, with an average of 0.51 g·g−1 water uptake per cycle. The regenerated PWCD can be used again for next water harvest from plant transpiration. These results demonstrate that the moisture from plant transpiration can be successfully utilized for water harvest. Considering the obtained amount of fresh water, if a large tree or a large area of plants were utilized, a substantial amount of water would be produced continuously. For example, a mature Q. emoryi tree can transpire 50 L per day [44], meeting the daily drinking water needs of at least 20 people. Additionally, the transpiration capacity can reach approximately 7.6 × 106 L of water per hectare per year at the Texas [45], which could serve as an important source of clean water.

In addition to water harvesting, PWCD can also help preserve plant freshness. Postharvest plants often release large amounts of water vapor, creating localized high-humidity microenvironments that accelerate spoilage. With its excellent absorption–desorption capabilities and regenerative properties, PWCD offers a promising solution for in situ vapor capture to maintain plant freshness while simultaneously regenerating to produce fresh water. As shown in Fig. 6 e, during strawberry storage, the RH increased to 96% within 5 h and stayed near saturation for the following 82 h, leading to rapid spoilage (indicated by white circles). In contrast, strawberries stored with PWCD maintained RH within the range of 50%-70%. Even after 82 h, the strawberries and blueberries remained fresh, significantly extending their shelf life. (Fig. 6 g and Fig. S 26). During sorbents regeneration, PWCD exhibits stable performance over 4 adsorption/desorption cycles, with each cycle collected water yield of 0.45 g·g−1 (Fig. 6 f). These results demonstrate the significant potential of PWCD in addressing the formidable challenge of water resource shortage from plant transpiration while keeping plant fresh.

In summary, we synthesized a green PWCD with low toxicity, high moisture absorption, high photothermal conversion, fast desorption, excellent cyclic stability and antibacterial properties via a simple one-step hydrothermal method, serving as a novel adsorbent for water collection from plant transpiration. With many phosphate groups on the shell as the water absorption nucleation site, it can absorb 0.11–3.23 gwater/gabsorbents water at 10%–90% RH. Meanwhile, PWCD can desorb 80% of water within 36 min at 75 °C, which is even much faster than that of pure water evaporation. Furthermore, PWCD also shows excellent photothermal conversion ability, with final photothermal temperature surpass 100 °C. With unique wrapping structures of PWCD, the excellent redispersability is preserved. We were able to collect 0.51 g·g−1 of water per cycle near Epipremnum aureum while keeping plant fresh, respectively. By virtue of its efficiency, sustainability, biocompatibility, and environmental friendliness, this adsorbent material not only addresses technological gaps but also unlocks practical application potentials for AWH systems and provides a new pathway for AWH.
